title: Relhania biennis
taxonomy: Relhania biennis is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. It belongs to the genus Relhania, which is a group of flowering plants native to the western and southern parts of the Cape Province in South Africa. The genus Relhania is further classified under the tribe Gnaphalieae, a group of flowering plants that are characterized by their small, daisy-like flowers and hairy stems. Relhania biennis is a biennial plant, meaning it takes two years to complete its life cycle, and is typically found growing in well-drained soils in sunny areas. The plant's classification reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history as a member of the Asteraceae family.

intro: Relhania biennis, commonly known as the Cape daisy or King's cabbage, is a shrubby plant native to the coastal regions of South Africa, belonging to the Asteraceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to alleviate various health issues, including digestive problems, inflammation, and respiratory issues, due to its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Relhania biennis include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Relhania biennis is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and ointments for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects.
